llcFilesWith: Alabama Secretary of State (Application to Register or Renew a Trademark, Service Mark, or Trade Name, Form LAT-1)
soleProprietorFilesWith: Alabama Secretary of State as well, on the same trade name application; Alabama has no mandatory DBA filing, so registration is optional for any owner
feeBasis: The $30 Secretary of State fee registers a trade name for five years (a $1.20 processing fee applies online). Registration is voluntary; Alabama does not require a business to register a name it trades under.
renewalRule: Renewable for successive five-year terms; a renewal application must be filed within six months before the registration expires while the name is still in use.
protectionNote: Alabama is unusual in having no separate assumed-name or DBA statute; the only way to register a name you trade under is to register it as a trade name under the trademark chapter (Title 8, Chapter 12). That registration is more than disclosure: it gives the registrant a claim of ownership in the mark and access to the infringement remedies in § 8-12-16, so it functions like a state trademark rather than a bare notice filing. It sits in the trademark register, separate from the entity-name distinguishability records used at formation.
searchNote: Registered trade names and marks are searched in the Secretary of State's trademark records, which are separate from the business-entity records. Because Alabama does not require DBA registration, an unregistered trade name in actual use will not appear in either search.
notes: Alabama has no mandatory DBA system. A business that wants to secure a name it trades under registers it as a trade name with the Secretary of State under the trademark chapter for $30, good for five years and renewable, and that registration carries trademark-style protection rather than mere disclosure. This is the reverse of most states, where DBA registration is required but confers no rights: in Alabama it is optional but does confer rights.
